findings.title: yaml.github-actions.security.github-actions-mutable-action-tag.github-actions-mutable-action-tag: GitHub Actions step uses a mutable tag or branch reference. Tags and branch names can be silently repointed by the action owner, enabling supply-chain attacks — as seen in the trivy-action and kics-github-action compromises. Pin the reference to a full 40-character commit SHA instead, e.g. `uses: actions/checkout@8ade135a41bc03ea155e62e844d188df1ea18608`.
findings.description: Semgrep rule yaml.github-actions.security.github-actions-mutable-action-tag.github-actions-mutable-action-tag detected: GitHub Actions step uses a mutable tag or branch reference. Tags and branch names can be silently repointed by the action owner, enabling supply-chain attacks — as seen in the trivy-action and kics-github-action compromises. Pin the reference to a full 40-character commit SHA instead, e.g. `uses: actions/checkout@8ade135a41bc03ea155e62e844d188df1ea18608`.

attack_scenario.actor: An attacker who can compromise the GitHub Actions workflow or the action owner's account to repoint a mutable tag/branch to malicious code.
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker identifies a GitHub Actions step using a mutable tag (e.g., `actions/checkout@v3`) in `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` line 14. 2. Attacker compromises the action owner's repository or account and repoints the tag `v3` to a malicious commit. 3. On the next workflow run, the malicious action executes, potentially exfiltrating secrets or modifying the build.
attack_scenario.impact: Supply-chain compromise: attacker can execute arbitrary code in the CI/CD pipeline, steal secrets (e.g., AWS keys, deployment tokens), or inject backdoors into the deployed artifact.
attack_scenario.explanation: The finding correctly identifies a mutable tag reference in a GitHub Actions workflow. This is a real supply-chain risk as demonstrated by real-world compromises (e.g., trivy-action, kics-github-action). The fix is to pin to a full commit SHA.

findings.cwe: CWE-1357: Reliance on Insufficiently Trustworthy Component
findings.tool_output: Rule: yaml.github-actions.security.github-actions-mutable-action-tag.github-actions-mutable-action-tag | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures A08:2025 - Software and Data Integrity Failures]

findings.title: semgrep.ironwall.xss.flask-request-to-response: User input from request.form.get flows to a string response without visible HTML escaping. Use render_template() or markupsafe.escape().
findings.description:
Semgrep rule semgrep.ironwall.xss.flask-request-to-response detected: User input from request.form.get flows to a string response without visible HTML escaping. Use render_template() or markupsafe.escape().
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — The finding is a duplicate of IRON-SEMGREP-003 through IRON-SEMGREP-005 (same file, same line). Additionally, the code snippet only shows 'requires login' which is insufficient to determine if user input is actually reflected unsafely. Without seeing the actual route handler, this is a false positive due to lack of context.]

findings.title: python.flask.security.open-redirect.open-redirect: Data from request is passed to redirect(). This is an open redirect and could be exploited. Consider using 'url_for()' to generate links to known locations. If you must use a URL to unknown pages, consider using 'urlparse()' or similar and checking if the 'netloc' property is the same as your site's host name. See the references for more information.
findings.description:
Semgrep rule python.flask.security.open-redirect.open-redirect detected: Data from request is passed to redirect(). This is an open redirect and could be exploited. Consider using 'url_for()' to generate links to known locations. If you must use a URL to unknown pages, consider using 'urlparse()' or similar and checking if the 'netloc' property is the same as your site's host name. See the references for more information.
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — The finding is at line 149, but the code shown is 'requires login' which is a comment. Without the actual code, we cannot confirm open redirect. Additionally, if the redirect uses request.url or url_for, it would be a false positive per rules.]

attack_scenario.explanation: The finding is at line 149, but the code shown is 'requires login' which is a comment. Without the actual code, we cannot confirm open redirect. Additionally, if the redirect uses request.url or url_for, it would be a false positive per rules.

findings.title: html.security.audit.missing-integrity.missing-integrity: This tag is missing an 'integrity' subresource integrity attribute. The 'integrity' attribute allows for the browser to verify that externally hosted files (for example from a CDN) are delivered without unexpected manipulation. Without this attribute, if an attacker can modify the externally hosted resource, this could lead to XSS and other types of attacks. To prevent this, include the base64-encoded cryptographic hash of the resource (file) you’re telling the browser to fetch in the 'integrity' attribute for all externally hosted files.
findings.description: Semgrep rule html.security.audit.missing-integrity.missing-integrity detected: This tag is missing an 'integrity' subresource integrity attribute. The 'integrity' attribute allows for the browser to verify that externally hosted files (for example from a CDN) are delivered without unexpected manipulation. Without this attribute, if an attacker can modify the externally hosted resource, this could lead to XSS and other types of attacks. To prevent this, include the base64-encoded cryptographic hash of the resource (file) you’re telling the browser to fetch in the 'integrity' attribute for all externally hosted files.

attack_scenario.actor: Attacker who can compromise the CDN or perform a man-in-the-middle attack on the CDN delivery path.
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker compromises the CDN serving the external script at line 7 of base.html. 2. Attacker modifies the script to include malicious JavaScript. 3. User's browser loads the modified script without integrity check. 4. Malicious script executes in user's session, potentially stealing cookies or performing actions.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of any user visiting the site, leading to session hijacking, data theft, or defacement.
attack_scenario.explanation: Missing SRI on CDN scripts is a real supply chain risk. The finding is at line 7 of base.html, which is a template file, not a comment. This is a valid finding.

findings.cwe: CWE-353: Missing Support for Integrity Check
findings.tool_output: Rule: html.security.audit.missing-integrity.missing-integrity | Severity: WARNING | OWASP: [A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures A08:2025 - Software or Data Integrity Failures]

attack_scenario.actor: Attacker who can compromise the CDN or perform a man-in-the-middle attack on the CDN delivery path.
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker compromises the CDN serving the external script at line 55 of base.html. 2. Attacker modifies the script. 3. User's browser loads the modified script without integrity check. 4. Malicious script executes.

Attacker tricks a logged-in user into submitting the form (e.g., via phishing or XSS). 3. The form submission is processed without CSRF token validation, performing actions on behalf of the victim.

findings.title: Unpinned GitHub Action in deploy.yml
findings.description: GitHub Actions should be pinned to commit SHA, not version tags. Tags can be moved by attackers who compromise the action repository (GhostAction 2025 attack vector).
findings.fix_suggestion: Pin actions to full commit SHA: uses: actions/checkout@a81bb... instead of @v4

findings.title: OpenSSF Scorecard not installed

findings.title: Missing 3 security controls in register
findings.description: Handler register is missing 3 controls: input_validation, rate_limiting, csrf_protection. The handler does not validate the format of email or the length/character set of username before processing. This could lead to invalid data being stored or potential injection attacks. The registration endpoint is not rate-limited, allowing an attacker to create many accounts rapidly (automated registration attacks). The registration form uses POST but does not include a CSRF token. This makes it vulnerable to cross-site request forgery attacks where an attacker can force a user to register without their consent.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add validation for email format (e.g., using a regex or a library like email-validator) and enforce constraints on username (e.g., length, allowed characters).; Implement rate limiting on the registration route, e.g., using Flask-Limiter or a similar middleware.; Include a CSRF token in the registration form and validate it on the server side. In Flask, use Flask-WTF or include a hidden token field.

findings.title: Missing 3 security controls in login
findings.description: Handler login is missing 3 controls: input_validation, rate_limiting, content_type_validation. The email and password fields are not validated for format, length, or content before being used in database queries and password verification. This could lead to unexpected behavior or security issues. The login endpoint does not implement rate limiting, allowing attackers to perform brute-force attacks without restriction. Although account lockout is implemented after 5 failed attempts, rate limiting would add an additional layer of protection. The handler does not validate the Content-Type header of incoming POST requests. While form-based login typically expects application/x-www-form-urlencoded, failing to validate could allow unexpected content types to be processed.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate email format using a library like email-validator, enforce minimum/maximum length on both fields, and sanitize inputs to prevent injection attacks.; Implement rate limiting using Flask-Limiter or similar middleware to restrict the number of login attempts per IP address or user account within a time window.; Add a check at the beginning of the POST handler to ensure Content-Type is 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded' or 'multipart/form-data'.

findings.title: Missing 4 security controls in upload_file
findings.description: Handler upload_file is missing 4 controls: input_validation, rate_limiting, csrf_protection, content_type_validation. The handler only checks file extension via allowed_file() and uses secure_filename(). It does not validate file content type (MIME), size, or perform any content inspection. An attacker could upload a malicious file with a valid extension. File upload endpoints are prone to abuse (DoS via large files, mass uploads). No rate limiting is applied, allowing an attacker to upload many files quickly. The handler accepts POST requests for file uploads but does not include CSRF token validation. This allows cross-site request forgery attacks where an attacker can trick a user into uploading files. The handler does not validate the Content-Type header of the uploaded file. An attacker could upload a file with a different MIME type than expected, potentially bypassing extension-based checks.
findings.code_snippet:
def upload_file():
    current_app.logger.info(f'UPLOAD endpoint hit by user {current_user.id}')
    if request.method == 'POST':
        if 'file' not in request.files:
            flash('No file part')
            return redirect(request.url)

        file = request.files['file']
        if file.filename == '':
            flash('No selected file')
            return redirect(request.url)

        if file and allowed_file(file.filename):
            filename = secure_filename(file.filename)
            file_path = os.path.join(
                current_app.config['UPLOAD_FOLDER'], filename)

            # Read file content and compute hash
            file_content = file.read()
            file_hash = sha256(file_content).hexdigest()
            current_app.logger.info(
                f'Computed SHA-256 hash for upload "{filename}": {file_hash}')

            # Encrypt the file content
            encrypted_content = current_app.config['CIPHER_SUITE'].encrypt(
                file_content)

            # Save the encrypted file
            with open(file_path, 'wb') as f:
                f.write(encrypted_content)

            # Save metadata with hash
            new_file = File(filename=filename,
                            user_id=current_user.id, file_hash=file_hash)
            db.session.add(new_file)
            db.session.commit()

            flash('File successfully uploaded')
            current_app.logger.info(
                f'User {current_user.id} uploaded "{filename}"')
            return redirect(url_for('main.index'))

        flash('Invalid file')
        return redirect(request.url)

    return render_template('upload.html')
findings.fix_suggestion: Add MIME type validation using python-magic or similar library. Also add file size limit check before reading entire file into memory.; Implement rate limiting using Flask-Limiter or similar. Limit to e.g., 10 uploads per minute per user.; Add CSRF protection using Flask-WTF or include a CSRF token in the form and validate it on POST.; Validate the Content-Type header against allowed MIME types (e.g., image/jpeg, application/pdf) before processing the file.

findings.title: Missing 4 security controls in share_file
findings.description: Handler share_file is missing 4 controls: auth, input_validation, rate_limiting, csrf_protection. The share_file handler does not verify that the current user is authenticated before allowing file sharing operations. Although the code references current_user, there is no explicit authentication check (e.g., login_required decorator or session validation). An unauthenticated user could potentially access this endpoint and share files. The file_id parameter is used directly in a database query without validation (e.g., type check, sanitization). The email parameter from the form is also used directly in a database query without format validation. This could lead to injection attacks or unexpected behavior. The share_file endpoint does not implement any rate limiting. An attacker could repeatedly call this endpoint to enumerate valid email addresses (by observing 'User not found' vs success) or to spam share invitations. The share_file handler processes POST requests to create file shares but does not include CSRF token validation. This makes the endpoint vulnerable to cross-site request forgery attacks, where an attacker could trick an authenticated user into sharing files without their consent.
findings.code_snippet:
def share_file(file_id):
    current_app.logger.info(
        f'SHARE page hit for file_id={file_id} by user {current_user.id}')
    file = File.query.get_or_404(file_id)
    if request.method == 'POST':
        email = request.form.get('email')
        user_to_share_with = User.query.filter_by(email=email).first()
        current_app.logger.debug(
            f'Sharing file_id={file_id} with email={email}')
        if not user_to_share_with:
            flash('User not found')
            current_app.logger.warning(
                f'Cannot share: user not found for email={email}')
            return redirect(request.url)

        # Prevent sharing with yourself
        if user_to_share_with.id == current_user.id:
            flash('You cannot share a file with yourself')
            return redirect(request.url)

        # Check if the file is already shared with this user
        existing_share = FileShare.query.filter_by(
            file_id=file.id, shared_with_user_id=user_to_share_with.id).first()
        if existing_share:
            flash('File already shared with this user')
            return redirect(request.url)

        # Create a new file share entry
        new_share = FileShare(
            file_id=file.id, shared_with_user_id=user_to_share_with.id)
        db.session.add(new_share)
        db.session.commit()

        current_app.logger.info(
            f'File {file_id} shared to user {user_to_share_with.id}')
        flash('File successfully shared')
        return redirect(url_for('main.index'))

    return render_template('share.html', file=file)
findings.fix_suggestion: Add the @login_required decorator from Flask-Login or implement an explicit authentication check at the start of the function, such as 'if not current_user.is_authenticated: abort(401)'.; Validate file_id as an integer (e.g., using int() with error handling). Validate email format using a regex or a library like email-validator before querying the database.; Implement rate limiting using Flask-Limiter or a similar middleware. For example, apply a decorator like @limiter.limit('10 per minute') to this endpoint.; Include a CSRF token in the form (e.g., using Flask-WTF's {{ form.hidden_tag() }}) and validate it on the server side. Alternatively, use Flask's built-in CSRF protection if enabled.

findings.title: Missing input validation on user ID
findings.description: The handler uses current_user.id directly in database queries without validating that it is a valid integer or sanitizing it. While current_user.id is typically trusted, lack of validation could lead to issues if the user object is tampered with or if the ID is unexpectedly modified.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add validation to ensure current_user.id is a positive integer before using it in queries. Example: if not isinstance(current_user.id, int) or current_user.id <= 0: abort(400)

findings.title: Missing 2 security controls in download_file
findings.description: Handler download_file is missing 2 controls: input_validation, rate_limiting. The filename parameter is used directly in file system operations (os.path.join) and database queries without validation. This could lead to path traversal or injection attacks if the filename is not properly sanitized. The download endpoint is not rate-limited. An attacker could repeatedly download files to exhaust server resources or perform a denial-of-service attack.
findings.code_snippet:
def download_file(filename):
    current_app.logger.info(
        f'DOWNLOAD request for "{filename}" by user {current_user.id}')
    file = File.query.filter_by(filename=filename).first_or_404()
    # Check if the current user is the owner or has been shared the file
    if file.user_id != current_user.id and not FileShare.query.filter_by(file_id=file.id, shared_with_user_id=current_user.id).first():
        current_app.logger.warning(
            f'Unauthorized download attempt by user {current_user.id} for file "{filename}"')
        flash('You do not have permission to access this file')
        return redirect(url_for('main.index'))

    file_path = os.path.join(current_app.config['UPLOAD_FOLDER'], filename)

    # Decrypt the file content
    with open(file_path, 'rb') as f:
        encrypted_content = f.read()
    decrypted_content = current_app.config['CIPHER_SUITE'].decrypt(
        encrypted_content)

    # Integrity check
    computed_hash = sha256(decrypted_content).hexdigest()
    current_app.logger.info(
        f'Computed SHA-256 hash for download "{filename}": {computed_hash}, expected: {file.file_hash}')
    if computed_hash != file.file_hash:
        current_app.logger.error(
            f'File integrity check failed for "{filename}" (user {current_user.id})')
        flash('File integrity verification failed. Download aborted.')
        return redirect(url_for('main.index'))

    # Send the decrypted content
    current_app.logger.info(
        f'Authorization OK — sending "{filename}" to user {current_user.id}')
    return send_file(BytesIO(decrypted_content), download_name=filename, as_attachment=True)
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate the filename parameter: ensure it does not contain path traversal sequences (../), null bytes, or special characters. Use a whitelist of allowed characters or a UUID-based naming scheme.; Implement rate limiting using Flask-Limiter or similar middleware. For example: @limiter.limit('10 per minute')

findings.title: Missing 2 security controls in download_file_token
findings.description: Handler download_file_token is missing 2 controls: input_validation, rate_limiting. The token parameter is used directly in database queries and file operations without validation for format, length, or character set. An attacker could potentially inject malicious input. No rate limiting is applied to this endpoint. An attacker could brute-force download tokens or exhaust server resources by making many requests.
findings.code_snippet:
def download_file_token(token):
    current_app.logger.info(
        f'DOWNLOAD-TOKEN hit with token={token[:8]}... by user {current_user.id}')
    download_token = DownloadToken.query.filter_by(token=token).first_or_404()
    if download_token.expires_at < datetime.utcnow():
        flash('Download link has expired')
        current_app.logger.warning(f'Token expired: {token[:8]}...')
        return redirect(url_for('main.index'))
    file = download_token.file
    # Only allow if current user is the token creator, file owner, or shared user
    is_owner = file.user_id == current_user.id
    is_token_user = download_token.user_id == current_user.id
    is_shared = FileShare.query.filter_by(
        file_id=file.id, shared_with_user_id=current_user.id).first() is not None
    if not (is_owner or is_token_user or is_shared):
        current_app.logger.warning(
            f'Unauthorized token use by user {current_user.id} for token={token[:8]}...')
        flash('You do not have permission to access this file')
        return redirect(url_for('main.index'))
    db.session.delete(download_token)
    db.session.commit()
    current_app.logger.info(
        f'Token consumed and file "{file.filename}" served to user {current_user.id}')

    file_path = os.path.join(
        current_app.config['UPLOAD_FOLDER'], file.filename)
    # Decrypt the file content
    with open(file_path, 'rb') as f:
        encrypted_content = f.read()
    decrypted_content = current_app.config['CIPHER_SUITE'].decrypt(
        encrypted_content)

    # Integrity check
    computed_hash = sha256(decrypted_content).hexdigest()
    current_app.logger.info(
        f'Computed SHA-256 hash for download "{file.filename}": {computed_hash}, expected: {file.file_hash}')
    if computed_hash != file.file_hash:
        current_app.logger.error(
            f'File integrity check failed for "{file.filename}" (user {current_user.id})')
        flash('File integrity verification failed. Download aborted.')
        return redirect(url_for('main.index'))

    # Send the decrypted content
    current_app.logger.info(
        f'Authorization OK — sending "{file.filename}" to user {current_user.id}')
    return send_file(BytesIO(decrypted_content), download_name=file.filename, as_attachment=True)
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate token format (e.g., UUID or alphanumeric string) and length before use. Consider using a regex pattern or type conversion.; Apply rate limiting (e.g., Flask-Limiter) to this endpoint, especially since it involves file decryption and serving.

findings.title: Missing 3 security controls in delete_file
findings.description: Handler delete_file is missing 3 controls: input_validation, rate_limiting, csrf_protection. The file_id parameter is used directly in File.query.get_or_404(file_id) without any validation. While get_or_404 handles missing records, it does not validate the format or type of file_id, which could lead to unexpected behavior or injection if the parameter is malformed. The delete_file endpoint does not implement rate limiting, allowing an attacker to repeatedly delete files in rapid succession, potentially causing denial of service or data loss. The delete_file handler performs a state-changing operation (DELETE) but does not include CSRF token validation. This could allow cross-site request forgery attacks where an attacker tricks a user into deleting files without their consent.
findings.code_snippet:
def delete_file(file_id):
    file = File.query.get_or_404(file_id)
    # Only the owner can delete
    if file.user_id != current_user.id:
        flash('You do not have permission to delete this file')
        return redirect(url_for('main.index'))
    # Delete file from filesystem
    file_path = os.path.join(
        current_app.config['UPLOAD_FOLDER'], file.filename)
    if os.path.exists(file_path):
        os.remove(file_path)
    # Delete related shares and tokens
    FileShare.query.filter_by(file_id=file.id).delete()
    DownloadToken.query.filter_by(file_id=file.id).delete()
    db.session.delete(file)
    db.session.commit()
    current_app.logger.info(
        f'File {file_id} fully deleted by user {current_user.id}')
    flash('File deleted successfully')
    return redirect(url_for('main.index'))
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate file_id is an integer or UUID before querying. Example: if not isinstance(file_id, int): abort(400).; Add rate limiting middleware or decorator. Example: @limiter.limit('10 per minute').; Add CSRF token validation. In Flask, use CSRFProtect extension or include a CSRF token in the form and validate it before processing the request.

findings.title: Hardcoded SQLite database URI
findings.description: The SQLite database URI is hardcoded as 'sqlite:///site.db' in create_app. This is a default configuration that may be insecure in production, but more importantly, it reveals the database type and location. However, the primary concern is that the secret key is loaded from environment variable, which is good, but the database URI being hardcoded could lead to information disclosure.
findings.code_snippet:
def create_app():
    app = Flask(__name__)
    encryption_key = os.getenv('ENCRYPTION_KEY')
    if not encryption_key:
        raise ValueError("No ENCRYPTION_KEY set for Flask application")
    cipher_suite = Fernet(encryption_key)
    app.config['CIPHER_SUITE'] = cipher_suite

    app.secret_key = os.environ['FLASK_SECRET_KEY']
    app.config['SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI'] = 'sqlite:///site.db'
    app.config['UPLOAD_FOLDER'] = os.path.join(app.root_path, '../uploads')

    db.init_app(app)
    login_manager.init_app(app)

    # Import models after initializing extensions
    from .models import User  # Move this import here

    # Import blueprints after initializing extensions
    from .routes import main, auth
    app.register_blueprint(main)
    app.register_blueprint(auth, url_prefix='/auth')

    # ensure the logs directory exists
    if not os.path.exists('logs'):
        os.mkdir('logs')

    # set up a rotating file handler: 10 MB per file, keep 5 backups
    file_handler = RotatingFileHandler(
        filename='logs/app.log',
        maxBytes=10 * 1024 * 1024,
        backupCount=5
    )
    # choose a nice format
    file_handler.setFormatter(logging.Formatter(
        '%(asctime)s %(levelname)s: %(message)s '
        '[in %(pathname)s:%(lineno)d]'
    ))
    file_handler.setLevel(logging.INFO)

    # attach to Flask's logger
    app.logger.addHandler(file_handler)

    # optionally set the overall log level (default is WARNING)
    app.logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)
    app.logger.info('Application startup')

    return app
findings.fix_suggestion: Move the database URI to an environment variable, e.g., app.config['SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI'] = os.environ.get('DATABASE_URL', 'sqlite:///site.db')

findings.title: Session cookies missing Secure and HttpOnly flags
findings.description: The Flask app does not explicitly set session cookie flags like Secure, HttpOnly, or SameSite. By default, Flask sets HttpOnly on session cookies, but Secure is not set unless the app is running over HTTPS. The code does not configure SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE, SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY, or SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE, which could lead to session hijacking if not using HTTPS.
findings.code_snippet:
def create_app():
    app = Flask(__name__)
    encryption_key = os.getenv('ENCRYPTION_KEY')
    if not encryption_key:
        raise ValueError("No ENCRYPTION_KEY set for Flask application")
    cipher_suite = Fernet(encryption_key)
    app.config['CIPHER_SUITE'] = cipher_suite

    app.secret_key = os.environ['FLASK_SECRET_KEY']
    app.config['SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI'] = 'sqlite:///site.db'
    app.config['UPLOAD_FOLDER'] = os.path.join(app.root_path, '../uploads')

    db.init_app(app)
    login_manager.init_app(app)

    # Import models after initializing extensions
    from .models import User  # Move this import here

    # Import blueprints after initializing extensions
    from .routes import main, auth
    app.register_blueprint(main)
    app.register_blueprint(auth, url_prefix='/auth')

    # ensure the logs directory exists
    if not os.path.exists('logs'):
        os.mkdir('logs')

    # set up a rotating file handler: 10 MB per file, keep 5 backups
    file_handler = RotatingFileHandler(
        filename='logs/app.log',
        maxBytes=10 * 1024 * 1024,
        backupCount=5
    )
    # choose a nice format
    file_handler.setFormatter(logging.Formatter(
        '%(asctime)s %(levelname)s: %(message)s '
        '[in %(pathname)s:%(lineno)d]'
    ))
    file_handler.setLevel(logging.INFO)

    # attach to Flask's logger
    app.logger.addHandler(file_handler)

    # optionally set the overall log level (default is WARNING)
    app.logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)
    app.logger.info('Application startup')

    return app
findings.fix_suggestion: Add configuration: app.config['SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE'] = True (if HTTPS), app.config['SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY'] = True, app.config['SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE'] = 'Lax' or 'Strict'
